data.claim: Random Forest substantially outperforms logistic regression for predicting civil war onset on the Muchlinski 2016 civil-war panel dataset.

data.name: Imputation-before-split leakage
data.reason: KNN imputation fits on the full dataframe before train/test split. Given ~20% MNAR missingness, this is the most likely source of an inflated RF advantage.

data.name: Target column included in imputation matrix
data.reason: If the target column is in the dataframe at impute time, KNN uses y as a neighbor-matching feature. Worth checking alongside H1.

data.name: Class-weight or sampling mismatch vs the paper
data.reason: Muchlinski uses class-balancing. If the repo's RF has class_weight='balanced' but LR does not (or vice versa), the gap is partly a tuning artifact.

data.name: Temporal structure ignored in split
data.reason: Panel is country-year. A random split (vs time-based) could be inflating both models if years leak.

data.description: Read src/prepare_data.py; find where the imputer is fit; confirm order relative to train_test_split.

data.result: prepare_data.py line 28: `imputer.fit_transform(df)` called on the full dataframe. train_test_split happens later on the imputed frame. Confirmed leakage.

data.description: Inspect what columns are in `df` when imputation runs. Confirm whether target is present.

data.result: prepare_data.py line 24 drops only ID_COLS before imputation; TARGET remains in the dataframe. KNN uses target as a feature during neighbor matching. Second confirmed leak.

data.reason_delta: Confirmed by direct inspection of prepare_data.py lines 24-28.
Confirmed: target is included in the imputation matrix.

data.summary: Compound imputation leakage. (1) KNN imputation is fit on the full dataframe before split, leaking test-feature distributions into training. (2) The target column is present in the imputation matrix, so KNN uses y as a neighbor-matching feature, directly leaking labels into imputed values.

data.diff_summary: Drop target and ID columns up front; split first; fit KNNImputer on X_train only; apply fitted imputer's transform to X_test.

data.context: Logistic Regression on held-out test set
Random Forest on held-out test set

data.markdown: The repo's reported AUCs were RF=0.9562, LR=0.8091 on a 25% stratified hold-out of the civil-war-onset panel. The implied research claim is that Random Forest substantially outperforms a Logistic Regression baseline (~15 AUC points).

Direct inspection of `src/prepare_data.py` (lines 24–28) confirmed two data-leakage patterns. The KNN imputer is fit on the entire dataframe before the train/test split, and the target column (`civil_war_onset`) is present in that dataframe at fit time. Both `fit_transform` invocations therefore use test-set feature values AND target values to compute imputed values that are then used in training.
Compound leakage via imputation. Fixing either half in isolation is insufficient.

Refactored `prepare(df)` to drop target + ID columns up front, call `train_test_split` on the raw feature matrix, then fit `KNNImputer` on X_train only and apply `transform` to X_test. Total diff: 14 lines changed in one file.

The synthetic dataset has MNAR missingness (onset=1 rows more missing than onset=0). On real Muchlinski data, the dominant mechanism may be MNAR-only, without the additional target-in-imputer pattern. We did not verify against the original R code's behavior beyond confirming the shape of the leakage in the Python port. The honest LR AUC (0.6962) is also likely to depend on seed choice — cross-validating over 5 seeds would tighten the estimate.
